meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Fabric Data Engineer Associate (DP-700)
meta.title: MS-FDE-1: Ingestion, Lakehouse, Real-Time Analytics and Governance

questions.stem: Which Fabric storage layer underpins lakehouses, warehouses, and other Fabric data experiences?

options.explanation: OneLake is the single, tenant-wide storage layer that underpins lakehouses, warehouses, eventhouses, and every other Fabric data experience — all items read from and write to the same unified lake.
Data Activator is an event-driven action service that triggers alerts and workflows based on streaming data conditions; it is a compute/automation layer, not a storage layer.
Power Query M is the transformation language used by Dataflows Gen2 and the Power Query editor; it is a programming language, not a storage system.
Deployment pipelines are a release-management feature for promoting Fabric content between development, test, and production workspaces; they do not provide storage.

questions.stem: Which Fabric item is designed to orchestrate Copy Data, Notebook, and Stored Procedure activities?

options.explanation: Fabric data pipelines are the orchestration surface that lets engineers chain Copy Data, Notebook, Stored Procedure, Script, and other activity types into sequenced, conditional workflows.
Semantic models are analytical query models used by Power BI reports; they serve DAX queries and do not orchestrate data movement or execution activities.
Real-Time Dashboards display live KQL query results from Eventhouse data; they are a visualization surface, not an orchestration tool.
The Capacity Metrics app is an administrative monitoring application for tracking CU utilization and throttling; it does not orchestrate data workflows.

questions.stem: Which Fabric feature is best for low-code transformations using Power Query M?

options.explanation: Eventhouse is the real-time analytical store for KQL-based event and log data; it does not provide a Power Query transformation surface.
Warehouse views are SQL objects (T-SQL) defined in a Fabric Data Warehouse; they use SQL, not Power Query M.
Dataflows Gen2 expose the Power Query visual editor where engineers author transformations in M, benefiting from over 150 connectors and a low-code experience that generates M behind the scenes.
Git integration connects Fabric workspaces to GitHub or Azure DevOps for version control and collaborative change management; it is a CI/CD feature, not a transformation tool.

questions.reference: [Differences between Dataflow Gen1 and Dataflow Gen2 - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/dataflows-gen2-overview)

questions.stem: A team needs to perform large-scale custom transformations on Delta data with PySpark. Which Fabric capability fits best?

options.explanation: Spark notebooks are the primary Fabric surface for writing and running PySpark, Scala, R, and SparkSQL code at scale against Delta tables in a lakehouse.
Deployment pipelines promote content between workspace stages (dev, test, prod); they do not execute transformations or run Spark code.
Sensitivity labels are Microsoft Purview governance metadata applied to Fabric items for classification purposes; they do not perform data transformation.
The Workspace Viewer role is a permission level that grants read-only access to workspace content; it is an access-control setting, not a transformation capability.

questions.stem: When choosing between a Lakehouse and a Warehouse in Fabric, which statement is most accurate?

options.explanation: Fabric Warehouses are SQL-centric and fully support T-SQL including SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, CREATE TABLE, and stored procedures.

options.text: Lakehouses are Spark-first over Delta files, while Warehouses are SQL-centric analytical stores
options.explanation: Lakehouses emphasize Spark-based engineering over Delta files and provide a SQL analytics endpoint as a secondary query surface, whereas Data Warehouses are the SQL-centric experience with full T-SQL DDL/DML support designed for dimensional warehousing.

options.text: Lakehouses cannot expose a SQL endpoint
options.explanation: Every Fabric Lakehouse automatically exposes a SQL analytics endpoint that allows read-only T-SQL queries over its managed Delta tables.
Spark notebooks are not restricted to Warehouses; notebooks are natively associated with Lakehouses in Fabric's Data Engineering experience.

questions.stem: What does the Fabric Capacity Metrics app primarily help a data engineer monitor?

options.explanation: Notebook syntax errors are surfaced in the notebook editor itself or the Spark execution output; the Capacity Metrics app does not parse or display code errors.

The Capacity Metrics app shows CU utilization over time, identifies which Fabric workloads are consuming the most resources, highlights throttling events, and helps administrators decide whether to scale capacity up or reschedule heavy workloads.
Endorsement status (Promoted, Certified) is shown in workspace item lists and the data hub; the Capacity Metrics app focuses on compute resource consumption, not governance metadata.
Workspace membership is managed in workspace settings; the Capacity Metrics app does not display or manage user roles.

questions.reference: [What is the Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/enterprise/metrics-app)

questions.stem: Which Delta maintenance actions are commonly used in Fabric Lakehouses to improve file layout and clean up old files?

options.explanation: BACKUP and RESTORE are database backup/recovery commands; Delta Lake uses transaction logs for time travel rather than traditional backup commands for file-layout maintenance.
GRANT and REVOKE are SQL access-control commands for managing object permissions; they do not affect Delta file layout or clean up obsolete files.

OPTIMIZE compacts many small Delta files into fewer larger files to improve read performance, while VACUUM removes files that are no longer referenced by the Delta transaction log (older than the retention threshold), freeing storage space.
PIVOT and UNPIVOT are T-SQL query operators for reshaping result sets between row and column orientations; they are not Delta maintenance commands.

questions.stem: A Fabric pipeline must ingest data incrementally from a source system. Which pattern is most appropriate?

options.text: Reload the entire source every minute
options.explanation: Reloading the full source every minute is extremely resource-intensive for both the source system and the Fabric capacity; it negates all benefits of an incremental strategy.

options.text: Track watermarks or change data and load only new or changed records
options.explanation: Incremental patterns use a watermark (such as a last-modified timestamp or a high-water mark ID) or change data capture (CDC) markers to identify and load only the records that are new or updated since the previous run, dramatically reducing data volume and processing time.
Disabling monitoring removes operational visibility into pipeline health; it does not improve ingestion latency and makes it harder to detect failures.

options.text: Use endorsement labels as offset markers
options.explanation: Endorsement labels are governance metadata (Promoted, Certified) for content discoverability; they are not data markers and cannot track ingestion state or offsets.

questions.stem: What is the Fabric Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint used for?

options.text: Read-only SQL queries over lakehouse tables
options.explanation: The SQL analytics endpoint provides a read-only T-SQL interface over the lakehouse's managed Delta tables, enabling SQL analysts to run SELECT queries using familiar syntax without running Spark.
Streaming ingestion from Kafka is handled by Eventstream (and the Eventhouse), not the lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint.
Tenant admin settings are managed in the Fabric Admin portal; the SQL analytics endpoint is a data query surface, not an admin configuration interface.
Publishing Power BI apps is performed in the Power BI service workspace; the SQL analytics endpoint has no role in app publishing.

questions.reference: [What is the SQL analytics endpoint for a lakehouse? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-engineering/lakehouse-sql-analytics-endpoint)

questions.stem: What is the main value of right-sizing Fabric capacities?

options.explanation: Capacity sizing has no effect on security controls; workspace roles, RLS, OLS, and sensitivity labels are all independent of which capacity SKU is used.

options.text: Balancing performance and cost based on actual workload demand
options.explanation: Choosing the right Fabric SKU ensures that peak workloads complete within acceptable time without paying for excess CU headroom that sits idle during off-peak hours — the core cost/performance trade-off in capacity planning.

options.text: Removing the need for Delta optimization
options.explanation: Delta table optimization (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, statistics collection) is a data engineering practice that remains necessary regardless of capacity size; larger capacity does not eliminate the need for good table layout.
Capacity sizing does not change which Fabric orchestration tools are used; pipelines and notebooks coexist and serve different purposes regardless of capacity tier.

questions.reference: [What is the Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/enterprise/metrics-app)

questions.stem: A Fabric warehouse is usually preferred over a lakehouse when the primary consumer needs which experience?

options.text: A SQL-centric warehousing experience with T-SQL DDL/DML
options.explanation: The Fabric Data Warehouse provides full T-SQL DDL/DML support including CREATE TABLE, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, stored procedures, and cross-database queries — the right fit when the primary consumer is a SQL analyst team that needs a complete warehousing experience.
Notebook-first Spark workflows are the core use case for a Lakehouse, not a Warehouse; warehouses do not natively surface a Spark notebook interface.
Power Query-only workflows are served by Dataflows Gen2, which is a separate Fabric item distinct from both Lakehouses and Warehouses.
KQL-only event analysis workflows are served by Eventhouse (backed by Azure Data Explorer technology) and Real-Time Dashboards, not a Data Warehouse.

questions.stem: Which Fabric real-time item ingests and routes streaming events from sources such as Event Hubs or Kafka?

options.explanation: Eventstream is the Fabric item purpose-built for capturing streaming events from sources such as Azure Event Hubs, Apache Kafka, IoT Hub, and custom endpoints, and routing them to destinations like Eventhouse, Lakehouse, or other Fabric items.
Dataflows Gen2 are batch-oriented Power Query transformations; they are not designed for continuous streaming ingestion from event brokers.
Deployment pipelines are a release-management feature for moving Fabric content between workspace stages; they do not ingest or route streaming events.
Semantic models serve analytical DAX queries from Power BI reports; they do not handle event ingestion or streaming routing.

questions.stem: Which query language is primarily used with Fabric Eventhouse data?

options.explanation: DAX (Data Analysis Expressions) is the formula language for Power BI semantic models and calculated columns/measures; it is not used to query Eventhouse data.
Power Query M is the transformation language for Dataflows Gen2 and the Power Query editor; it is not the query language for Eventhouse.

Eventhouse is built on Azure Data Explorer technology, and KQL (Kusto Query Language) is its native query language — a pipe-based language optimized for fast filtering, aggregation, and time-series analysis over high-volume event data.
MDX (Multidimensional Expressions) is the query language for traditional SSAS multidimensional cubes; it is not used with Fabric Eventhouse.

questions.stem: What is the main role of an Eventhouse in Fabric?

options.explanation: Power BI workbook controls are a feature of Power BI paginated reports and Excel integration; they have no connection to Eventhouse.

options.text: Store and query high-frequency real-time data
options.explanation: Eventhouse is the Fabric real-time analytical store designed to ingest, retain, and serve high-volume, high-frequency event and telemetry data with low-latency KQL queries.

options.text: Replace Git repositories for version control
options.explanation: Git repositories for version control are connected through Fabric's Git integration feature (GitHub, Azure DevOps); Eventhouse is a data store, not a source control system.
Semantic model scheduled refreshes are configured in the Power BI service workspace and executed by the Fabric capacity; Eventhouse does not manage or run semantic model refresh jobs.

questions.stem: Why would a data engineer configure pipeline alerts or notifications?

options.text: To automatically expand OneLake storage capacity
options.explanation: OneLake storage capacity is managed by the platform and scales independently; pipeline notifications do not trigger or configure storage expansion.

options.text: To detect failures or SLA risks quickly
options.explanation: Pipeline alerts and notification activities (such as sending an email or posting to a Teams channel on failure) reduce mean time to awareness, enabling on-call engineers to investigate and resolve issues before they breach SLAs or cascade into downstream reports.

options.text: To replace data quality validation checks
options.explanation: Data quality checks — such as row count assertions or null checks — are separate activities or notebook logic; notifications communicate outcomes but do not perform the validation themselves.

options.text: To create OneLake shortcuts on failure
options.explanation: OneLake shortcuts are created through the Fabric workspace UI or API; they cannot be created as a side-effect of a pipeline notification or alert.

questions.stem: A team wants to reference raw files already stored in ADLS Gen2 without copying them into a new Fabric location. Which feature is most appropriate?

options.explanation: A OneLake shortcut creates a virtual reference inside a Fabric Lakehouse that points directly to the ADLS Gen2 container; Fabric reads the files at their original location without copying or moving them.
Object-level security is a semantic model feature that restricts which tables or columns are visible to certain roles; it does not create storage access paths to external systems.
An Eventhouse query set stores saved KQL queries for exploring data in an Eventhouse; it does not virtualize access to external ADLS Gen2 files.
Deployment pipeline rules control how item parameters differ between dev, test, and production workspace stages; they do not provide connectivity or virtual access to external storage.

questions.reference: [Unify data sources with OneLake shortcuts - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/onelake/onelake-shortcuts)

questions.stem: What is a key advantage of connecting a Fabric workspace to GitHub or Azure DevOps?

options.text: It automatically increases Spark executor memory
options.explanation: Git integration is a source control feature; Spark executor memory is determined by the lakehouse or notebook compute configuration, not by Git connectivity.

options.text: It supports version control and collaboration for Fabric items
options.explanation: Fabric's Git integration synchronizes item definitions (notebooks, pipelines, semantic models, etc.) with a Git branch, enabling pull requests, code reviews, history tracking, and branching strategies for collaborative development.
Workspace roles (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer) are access-control assignments that remain in effect regardless of whether a workspace has Git integration enabled.
Git integration and deployment pipelines are complementary CI/CD features in Fabric; connecting a workspace to Git does not disable or interfere with deployment pipelines.

questions.stem: Which workspace role is most appropriate for a user who must create and edit content but does not need full workspace administration?

options.explanation: The Viewer role is read-only; a user with Viewer cannot create or edit workspace items, only consume them.

options.text: Contributor or Member, depending on the exact governance needs
options.explanation: Contributor allows creating and editing items in the workspace without the ability to manage workspace settings or add/remove members, while Member adds the ability to share items and manage permissions for specific items — the right range of roles for someone who needs authoring access without administration.
A user with no role cannot access any workspace items; they must have at least a Viewer role to see anything.
A tenant admin has organization-wide administrative capabilities in Fabric; granting tenant admin to a content creator vastly exceeds the access they need and violates least-privilege principles.

questions.reference: [Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/fundamentals/roles-workspaces)

questions.stem: What is the main value of Microsoft Purview integration with Fabric?

options.text: Replacing Delta tables with Purview-managed storage
options.explanation: Purview does not replace or manage Delta table storage; it scans and catalogs metadata about data assets, leaving the actual storage in OneLake.

Microsoft Purview integration brings data catalog discovery, end-to-end data lineage visualization, and sensitivity label application to Fabric items, helping organizations understand where data comes from, how it is used, and whether it meets compliance requirements.
Spark code execution is handled by the Fabric Spark compute engine inside notebooks and Spark job definitions; Purview is a governance and catalog tool, not a compute engine.
Power BI embed tokens are generated through the Power BI Embedding API for embedding reports in applications; Purview is not involved in token generation.

questions.reference: [Use Microsoft Purview to Govern Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/governance/microsoft-purview-fabric)

questions.stem: Why might a data engineer use medallion design in Fabric?

options.text: To eliminate the need for security
options.explanation: Medallion architecture is a data organization pattern; security controls (workspace roles, RLS, sensitivity labels) are still necessary and operate independently of which architectural pattern is used.

options.text: To force all queries through KQL
options.explanation: Medallion design places no restriction on query language; data in bronze, silver, or gold layers can be queried with T-SQL, Spark SQL, KQL, or DAX depending on which Fabric item exposes it.

options.text: To prevent SQL access to data
options.explanation: SQL access via the lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint is supported at any layer of a medallion design; the pattern does not restrict query engines.

options.text: To separate raw, refined, and curated data responsibilities across layers
options.explanation: The medallion pattern organizes data into bronze (raw as-landed), silver (cleaned and conformed), and gold (curated and aggregated) layers, giving each team a clear responsibility boundary and enabling progressive data quality improvement.

questions.stem: A source system occasionally sends late updates. Which incremental design handles this more safely than using only a monotonically increasing ID?

options.explanation: Ignoring late updates and waiting for a weekly full reload would leave inconsistent or stale data in the target for days, which is unacceptable for most operational or analytical workloads.

options.text: Use a watermark or change-tracking column that captures updates as well as inserts
options.explanation: Using a column like `last_modified_at` or a change-tracking indicator that is updated whenever a row changes (not just on insert) allows the pipeline to capture both new inserts and late-arriving updates — a monotonically increasing row ID would miss rows that were inserted earlier but updated after the last pipeline run.

options.text: Materialize a Real-Time Dashboard instead of loading data
options.explanation: A Real-Time Dashboard is a visualization surface backed by an Eventhouse; it does not solve the problem of capturing late-arriving updates in a batch ingestion pipeline.

options.text: Use endorsement labels to mark processed rows
options.explanation: Endorsement labels (Promoted, Certified) are metadata governance signals applied to Fabric items by administrators; they cannot mark individual data rows as processed and have no role in ingestion state tracking.

questions.stem: What is the main benefit of collecting Delta statistics and maintaining table layout?

options.text: Automatic generation of row-level security rules
options.explanation: Delta table statistics are used by the query engine for performance optimization; they have no connection to row-level security rule generation.

options.text: Improved query planning and data skipping opportunities
options.explanation: Delta column statistics (min/max values per file) allow the Spark engine to skip entire data files that cannot contain matching rows, reducing the amount of data read during query execution and improving planning accuracy.

options.text: Guaranteed zero compute cost for all queries
options.explanation: Query costs depend on data volume, query complexity, and capacity size; statistics help reduce unnecessary data reads but do not guarantee zero cost.

options.text: Elimination of the need for semantic models
options.explanation: Semantic models serve the analytical BI layer with measures, relationships, and security; Delta statistics are a storage-layer performance feature that does not replace the need for a governed semantic layer.

questions.stem: Which KQL operator family is commonly used for filtering and aggregating event data?

options.explanation: `grant` and `revoke` are SQL access-control statements used for managing database permissions; they are not KQL query operators used for filtering or aggregating event data.
`pivot` and `merge` are T-SQL query operators for reshaping result sets and joining data sets; while KQL has some similar operators, this is not the core filtering/aggregation family described.

`where` is the foundational KQL filtering operator that restricts rows to those matching a condition, and `summarize` is the aggregation operator that groups data and applies aggregate functions — together they are the most fundamental operators for event data analysis in KQL.
`widget` and `display` are not recognized KQL operators; they do not exist in the Kusto Query Language.

questions.stem: What is the purpose of Activator in Fabric real-time solutions?

options.text: Trigger actions or alerts from event conditions
options.explanation: Activator (formerly Data Activator) monitors data streams from Eventstream or Power BI and triggers configured actions — such as sending a Teams message, calling a webhook, or starting a pipeline — when defined conditions are met, enabling automated event-driven responses.
Power Query M transformations are authored in Dataflows Gen2 or the Power Query editor; Activator is not a transformation tool.
Delta tables are stored in Lakehouses within OneLake; Activator is an event-processing and alerting service, not a storage layer.

options.text: Replace KQL as the Eventhouse query language
options.explanation: KQL remains the native query language for Eventhouse; Activator works alongside Eventhouse and Eventstream to act on data conditions, but does not replace or modify KQL.

questions.stem: A workspace contains both sensitive engineering notebooks and broadly shared BI reports. Which approach best enforces least privilege?

options.explanation: Making all users workspace Admins grants every user the ability to modify workspace settings, add and remove members, and delete any item — the opposite of least privilege.

options.text: Disable Purview lineage to reduce visibility
options.explanation: Purview lineage is a governance and auditing feature; disabling it reduces observability and compliance capability without addressing access control.

options.text: Store everything in a single public app
options.explanation: Publishing everything in a single public app makes all content accessible to anyone who discovers the app link, which is incompatible with protecting sensitive engineering notebooks.

options.text: Assign appropriate workspace or item-level permissions and share only required items
options.explanation: Assigning the minimum workspace role each persona needs (e.g., Viewer for report consumers, Contributor for report authors, Member for senior engineers) and using item-level sharing for specific artifacts ensures each user has exactly the access they require and no more.

questions.reference: [Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/fundamentals/roles-workspaces)

questions.stem: Which Fabric feature records past pipeline executions for troubleshooting?

options.explanation: The pipeline run history view records each execution with its start/end time, overall status, and per-activity outcome, duration, and error details — giving engineers the information needed to diagnose which activity failed and why.
Calculation groups are a DAX semantic model construct for defining reusable calculation patterns like time intelligence; they have no role in pipeline execution tracking.
Object-level security is a semantic model feature that restricts which tables and columns are visible to certain roles; it does not record or surface pipeline execution history.
OneLake shortcuts are a storage virtualization feature that creates virtual references to external data locations; they do not log or track pipeline execution events.

questions.stem: Why might a Fabric engineer choose a notebook over Dataflows Gen2 for a transformation step?

options.text: Notebooks are the only Fabric feature that can be scheduled
options.explanation: Scheduling is not exclusive to notebooks; Dataflows Gen2, data pipelines, and Spark job definitions can all be scheduled independently.

options.text: Dataflows Gen2 cannot read any data from OneLake
options.explanation: Dataflows Gen2 can read from and write to OneLake via the Lakehouse connector; they have broad connectivity including to OneLake-backed sources.

options.text: Notebooks are required for all T-SQL statements
options.explanation: T-SQL statements can be run through the Data Warehouse editor or the lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint; notebooks are not required for T-SQL.

options.text: The transformation requires custom large-scale Spark logic
options.explanation: Notebooks are the appropriate choice when a transformation requires custom Spark logic, complex joins or window functions at scale, ML preprocessing, or any processing that exceeds the capabilities of the low-code Power Query editor in Dataflows Gen2.

questions.stem: Which statement about lakehouse tables in Fabric is accurate?

options.text: They cannot be queried with SQL
options.explanation: Lakehouse tables are queryable through the SQL analytics endpoint using T-SQL SELECT statements, in addition to being accessible via Spark SQL and PySpark APIs.

options.text: They are based on Delta table concepts and can be discovered by the lakehouse automatically
options.explanation: Fabric lakehouse managed tables are backed by the Delta Lake format and the lakehouse automatically discovers and registers Delta tables written into its Tables folder, making them queryable via Spark and the SQL analytics endpoint without manual schema registration.

options.text: They exist only inside Power BI dashboards
options.explanation: Lakehouse tables are data objects stored in OneLake; they exist independently of Power BI dashboards, which are visualization artifacts that may consume data from these tables.

options.text: They require an Eventhouse to function
options.explanation: Eventhouse is a separate Fabric item for real-time KQL analytics; it has no role in creating, managing, or serving Lakehouse Delta tables.

questions.stem: A notebook job is slow. Which Fabric surface is most useful for investigating stages, DAGs, and executor behavior?

options.explanation: Endorsement label settings (Promoted, Certified) are governance metadata applied to Fabric items; they provide no information about Spark execution stages, DAGs, or executor behavior.
The OneLake shortcut manager is used to view, create, and manage shortcut references to external storage locations; it does not expose Spark runtime diagnostics.

options.text: Spark monitoring details for the notebook run
options.explanation: The Spark monitoring view for a notebook run surfaces the Spark UI, showing job stages, task-level DAG visualization, executor metrics (memory, CPU, shuffle), and skew indicators — the information engineers need to identify bottlenecks in a slow notebook.
The deployment pipeline comparison view shows differences in item definitions between workspace stages (dev, test, prod); it does not display Spark execution details.

questions.stem: What is the main effect of sensitivity labels in Fabric?

options.text: They help classify and govern content based on data sensitivity
options.explanation: Sensitivity labels from Microsoft Purview Information Protection are applied to Fabric items to classify their data sensitivity level (e.g., Public, General, Confidential, Highly Confidential), enabling governance policies, audit reporting, and consistent classification across Microsoft 365 services.

options.text: They create Delta table checkpoints for time travel
options.explanation: Delta table checkpoints are created automatically by the Delta transaction log protocol to enable efficient log replay and time travel; they have no connection to sensitivity label application.

options.text: They optimize KQL query execution plans
options.explanation: KQL query optimization is handled internally by the Kusto engine based on table statistics and query structure; sensitivity labels have no effect on query execution plans.

options.text: They replace RLS and object-level permissions
options.explanation: Sensitivity labels are a classification and governance tool; they do not replace or enforce row-level security or object-level permissions, which are enforced by the Power BI/Fabric security engine at query time.

questions.reference: [Use Microsoft Purview to Govern Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/governance/microsoft-purview-fabric)

questions.stem: Why would a team use retry policies in Fabric pipelines?

options.text: To replace data validation and quality checks
options.explanation: Retry policies determine how many times a failed activity is attempted; they do not perform any data quality validation or row-level checks on the data being ingested.

options.text: To handle transient source or network failures automatically
options.explanation: Retry policies configure an activity to automatically re-attempt execution after a failure, with a specified retry count and wait interval between attempts — making pipelines more resilient to transient issues such as brief network timeouts or source system throttling.

options.text: To improve the DAX formula evaluation in semantic models
options.explanation: DAX formula evaluation is handled by the Power BI/Fabric semantic model engine and is entirely separate from pipeline activity configuration; retry settings have no effect on DAX performance.
Workspace provisioning is an administrative action performed through the Fabric admin portal or via REST API; it is not a function of pipeline retry policies.

questions.stem: Which Fabric item is best suited to low-latency dashboards directly over real-time event data?

options.explanation: Deployment pipelines are a release-management tool for promoting Fabric content between workspace stages; they are not a visualization surface for real-time data.

Power BI paginated reports (RDL format) are designed for formatted, pixel-perfect printed output with row-by-row detail; they are not optimized for low-latency, continuously refreshing dashboards over streaming event data.

options.text: A Real-Time Dashboard backed by Eventhouse
options.explanation: Real-Time Dashboards are designed specifically for low-latency visualization over Eventhouse data; they refresh automatically on short intervals and run KQL queries directly against the Eventhouse, making them ideal for live operational and telemetry dashboards.
A Git branch is a source control concept for managing code and item definition versions; it has no role as a data visualization or querying surface.

questions.stem: A team wants business analysts to join curated warehouse tables and lakehouse tables in a single analytical query path. Which Fabric capability makes this type of mixed analytical design possible?

options.explanation: Eventhouse retention policies control how long real-time event data is stored in a KQL database; they have no bearing on joining warehouse and lakehouse tables in SQL queries.

options.text: Fabric's shared OneLake platform with warehouse/lakehouse interoperability
options.explanation: Fabric's shared OneLake storage layer and cross-database querying capabilities allow SQL analytics on warehouse tables to reference lakehouse tables via shortcuts or cross-item SQL, enabling analysts to join data from both item types in a single query.
Deployment pipeline version tags track which version of items are deployed to each stage; they are a release-management metadata feature, not a query or join capability.
The Workspace Viewer role grants read access to workspace content; it is an access control assignment, not a technical capability that enables cross-item analytical queries.

questions.stem: Which role is most appropriate for a user who only needs to view workspace content?

options.explanation: The Viewer role is the read-only workspace role; users with this role can open and consume reports, dashboards, and other shared items but cannot create, edit, publish, or manage any content.
The Admin role provides full control over the workspace, including adding and removing members, modifying workspace settings, and deleting items — far more than a view-only consumer needs.
The Member role allows users to publish content, share items, and manage access for specific items in addition to viewing; this exceeds the permissions needed by a read-only consumer.
The Contributor role allows users to create, edit, and delete content in the workspace; this is authoring access, not view-only access.

questions.reference: [Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/fundamentals/roles-workspaces)

questions.stem: Which transformation surface is based on Power Query rather than Spark?

options.explanation: Notebooks in Fabric use the Spark compute engine and support PySpark, Scala, SparkSQL, and R; Power Query M is not a notebook language.
The Warehouse query editor runs T-SQL against the Fabric Data Warehouse; it uses the warehouse SQL engine, not Power Query.
The Eventstream processing capabilities use a streaming pipeline model for real-time event routing and transformation; it is not based on the Power Query M language.
Dataflows Gen2 are built on the Power Query engine, letting engineers author transformations visually in the Power Query editor with M generated behind the scenes.

questions.reference: [Differences between Dataflow Gen1 and Dataflow Gen2 - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/data-factory/dataflows-gen2-overview)

questions.stem: What is the most likely consequence of ignoring repeated capacity throttling warnings?

options.text: Performance degradation and delayed workloads may continue or worsen
options.explanation: Persistent capacity throttling means workloads are queuing or being deprioritized due to insufficient CUs; if left unaddressed, report queries slow down, pipeline runs are delayed, and SLAs may be missed — and the problem typically gets worse as more workloads compete for limited resources.
Capacity throttling is a resource management mechanism; it has no effect on semantic model security definitions such as row-level security, which remains enforced regardless of capacity state.

options.text: All lakehouse files are automatically deleted
options.explanation: OneLake storage is independent of capacity compute; throttling affects CU-bound compute workloads but does not trigger automatic deletion of lakehouse files.

options.text: Git history for workspace items is erased
options.explanation: Git history is managed in the external repository (GitHub or Azure DevOps); capacity throttling in Fabric has no impact on version control history.

questions.reference: [What is the Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/enterprise/metrics-app)

questions.stem: Which TWO Fabric capabilities are most directly involved in a real-time ingestion and action pipeline? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Eventstream captures events from sources such as Event Hubs and Kafka and routes them in real-time to destinations including Eventhouse, Lakehouse, and Activator.

Activator monitors data streams (from Eventstream or Power BI) and triggers configured actions — such as emails, Teams alerts, or pipeline invocations — when event conditions are met, completing the ingestion-to-action loop.
Deployment pipelines manage content promotion between workspace stages (dev, test, prod); they are an ALM tool with no role in real-time event ingestion or action triggering.
Calculation groups are a DAX semantic model construct for defining reusable time intelligence or other calculation patterns; they are unrelated to streaming event processing.

questions.stem: Which TWO features best support repeatable batch ingestion and transformation in Fabric? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Data pipelines orchestrate repeatable batch workflows, sequencing Copy Data, Notebook, Stored Procedure, and other activity types with scheduling, monitoring, and retry capabilities.
Notebooks provide scalable, schedulable Spark code for custom batch transformations — they can be triggered by pipelines or scheduled independently and write results to lakehouse Delta tables.
Endorsement labels (Promoted, Certified) communicate governance trust and curation quality to users; they are metadata, not tools that execute or orchestrate ETL logic.
Row-level security roles define which rows of a semantic model each user can see; they are a data access control feature and do not orchestrate or execute batch data engineering workflows.

questions.stem: Which TWO governance features most directly improve discoverability and compliance in Fabric? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Purview lineage shows how data flows from source to destination across Fabric items, and the Purview catalog makes data assets searchable with metadata, improving discoverability and supporting data governance audits.

Sensitivity labels classify Fabric items by data sensitivity level (e.g., Confidential, Highly Confidential), enabling compliance policies, audit trails, and consistent treatment across Microsoft 365 services.
Disabling Git integration removes version control and change history from Fabric items, reducing — not improving — governance and auditability.
Removing workspace roles would leave content unprotected and violate least-privilege access principles; proper role assignment is a foundation of data governance, not an obstacle to it.

questions.reference: [Use Microsoft Purview to Govern Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/governance/microsoft-purview-fabric)

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about OneLake shortcuts are accurate? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: Shortcuts create virtual references to existing data locations, so multiple Fabric items can access the same dataset without each team maintaining a separate physical copy — directly reducing storage duplication.

options.text: They can point to supported external storage such as ADLS Gen2
options.explanation: OneLake shortcuts support several external storage targets including ADLS Gen2, Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, and other OneLake paths within the same or different tenants.

options.text: They automatically replace all source-system access permissions
options.explanation: Source-system permissions are not automatically replaced or bypassed by a shortcut; the identity used to access the shortcut target still needs appropriate permissions on the external storage, and OneLake access control remains separate from source-system control.

options.text: They can only reference data stored in Eventhouse
options.explanation: Shortcuts are not limited to Eventhouse data; they can reference data in other Lakehouses, Warehouses, ADLS Gen2, Amazon S3, and more — Eventhouse is just one possible target among many.

questions.reference: [Unify data sources with OneLake shortcuts - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/onelake/onelake-shortcuts)

questions.stem: Which TWO KQL operators are foundational for shaping event data during analysis? (Select TWO)

options.explanation: `extend` adds one or more computed columns to the result set in a KQL query — for example, deriving a severity category from a numeric error code — without removing existing columns.
`project` selects and reorders a subset of columns from the input, reshaping the result set to include only the fields needed for the analysis and removing the rest.
`grant` is a SQL/KQL access-control statement for assigning permissions to database principals; it is an administrative command, not a data-shaping operator used during analytical query construction.
`vacuum` is a Delta Lake maintenance command (not a KQL operator) used to remove files older than the retention threshold from a Delta table; it has no role in KQL event data analysis.

questions.stem: Which TWO actions are most likely to improve cost and performance hygiene for Fabric engineering workloads? (Select TWO)

options.text: Right-size capacities based on actual workload demand
options.explanation: Choosing a capacity SKU that matches peak workload demand avoids paying for idle CUs during off-peak hours while ensuring peak jobs complete within acceptable time — the fundamental cost-performance balance in Fabric.

options.text: Monitor pipeline and Spark run details regularly
options.explanation: Reviewing pipeline run history and Spark monitoring output regularly helps engineers identify slow activities, data skew, and excessive resource consumption before they become persistent problems or SLA violations.

options.text: Disable all Purview lineage to reduce overhead
options.explanation: Purview lineage is a lightweight metadata feature with negligible compute overhead; disabling it removes valuable governance and auditability without meaningfully improving performance or reducing cost.

options.text: Avoid all Delta OPTIMIZE and VACUUM maintenance
options.explanation: Avoiding Delta maintenance leads to file fragmentation (many small files) and stale statistics, which degrade query performance over time — the opposite of good performance hygiene.

questions.reference: [What is the Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/enterprise/metrics-app)

questions.stem: Which TWO access controls are most important when a user should edit a specific item but not administer the full workspace? (Select TWO)

options.text: Appropriate workspace or item-level permissions scoped to what is needed
options.explanation: Granting access at the item level (rather than the workspace level) allows the user to create and edit only the specific artifact they need, without gaining visibility into or edit rights over every other item in the workspace.

The principle of least privilege requires assigning the minimum role that enables the required task — such as Contributor for the specific item rather than Member or Admin for the whole workspace — reducing the blast radius of accidental or malicious misuse.

options.text: Granting the user a tenant admin role
options.explanation: Tenant admin provides organization-wide administrative access to all Fabric capacities, workspaces, and settings — an extreme over-privilege for a user who only needs to edit one item.

options.text: Publishing the item publicly without access control
options.explanation: Publishing an item publicly without access control removes all security restrictions, making it visible and potentially editable by anyone — the antithesis of least-privilege access management.

questions.reference: [Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/fundamentals/roles-workspaces)

questions.stem: Which TWO techniques most directly support robust incremental ingestion in Fabric? (Select TWO)

options.text: Use watermarks or CDC indicators to track which records have been processed
options.explanation: Watermarks (high-water mark values such as last-modified timestamps or max IDs) and CDC change indicators allow the pipeline to request only the records that changed since the previous successful run, making ingestion efficient and avoiding redundant full reloads.

options.text: Track retry and monitoring outcomes to detect silent failures
options.explanation: Even a well-designed incremental pipeline can fail silently — a run may complete without error but miss rows due to a watermark miscalculation; monitoring run outcomes and retry behavior operationally catches these cases before they cause downstream data quality issues.

options.text: Reload the full source table on every pipeline run
options.explanation: Reloading the full source table on every run negates the entire point of incremental ingestion; it is wasteful for both the source system and Fabric capacity, especially as tables grow large.

options.text: Use endorsement labels on the target table to mark processed batches
options.explanation: Endorsement labels (Promoted, Certified) are governance metadata for Fabric items at the workspace level; they cannot be applied to individual rows in a table and have no role in tracking ingestion state or batch progress.

questions.stem: Why are OneLake shortcuts useful in medallion-style Fabric designs?

options.explanation: Shortcuts do not modify or replace workspace permissions; the permissions model for OneLake and the workspace remain governed through workspace roles and item-level sharing as usual.

options.text: They convert all referenced tables into Eventhouse KQL tables
options.explanation: Shortcuts create virtual references to Delta or file data in OneLake; they do not convert or migrate data to Eventhouse KQL tables, which use a completely different storage format.

options.text: They prevent SQL queries from reaching Delta tables
options.explanation: Shortcuts are transparent to query engines; SQL queries via the SQL analytics endpoint can reach Delta tables exposed through shortcuts just as they can reach natively stored tables.

options.text: They allow teams to reference existing raw data locations without duplicating files
options.explanation: Shortcuts let the silver or gold layer in a medallion design reference bronze layer data via a virtual path, so the raw files live in one location but can be accessed from multiple downstream lakehouses without duplicating the physical data.

questions.reference: [Unify data sources with OneLake shortcuts - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/onelake/onelake-shortcuts)

questions.stem: Which Fabric capability is best when an engineer needs to inspect and debug PySpark code interactively during development?

options.explanation: Notebook sessions in Fabric provide an interactive, cell-by-cell execution environment for PySpark code; engineers can run individual cells, inspect output and variable state, use display() to visualize DataFrames, and iterate on logic without restarting the entire Spark job.
Sensitivity labels are Microsoft Purview governance metadata applied to Fabric items for classification purposes; they have no role in interactive code development or debugging.

Deployment pipeline rules control how item parameter values differ between workspace stages during promotion; they are a release-management configuration tool, not a development or debugging environment.

Endorsement badges (Promoted, Certified) are discoverability metadata that signal the curation quality of Fabric items; they are applied by administrators and have no connection to interactive PySpark development.

questions.stem: What is the main reason to keep item-level sharing distinct from workspace-wide roles in Fabric?

options.text: It supports more granular least-privilege access to individual items
options.explanation: Item-level sharing allows an administrator or item owner to grant a specific user access to a single report, semantic model, or pipeline without giving them a workspace role that would expose every other item in the workspace — enabling a least-privilege approach when broad workspace access is not needed.

options.text: It increases Eventhouse data retention duration
options.explanation: Eventhouse data retention is configured through the KQL database retention policy settings; it is not affected by how workspace or item permissions are structured.

options.text: It disables Purview lineage tracking for shared items
options.explanation: Purview lineage tracking is a governance feature that runs independently of how permissions are assigned; item-level sharing does not disable or bypass lineage capture.

options.text: It removes the need for row-level security
options.explanation: Row-level security operates at the semantic model layer to restrict which data rows a user can see; item-level sharing controls which items a user can access at all — they address different layers of access control and neither eliminates the need for the other.

questions.reference: [Roles in workspaces in Microsoft Fabric - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/fundamentals/roles-workspaces)

questions.stem: Which real-time Fabric artifact is primarily a queryable store rather than an ingestion router?

options.explanation: Eventstream is the ingestion router — it captures streaming events from external sources and routes them to destinations; it does not serve as the persistent analytical store.
Deployment pipelines are a release-management tool for promoting Fabric content between workspace stages; they have no role in real-time data storage or querying.

Eventhouse is the persistent real-time analytical store in Fabric; it holds ingested event data in KQL databases and serves low-latency KQL queries against that stored data — the queryable store in the real-time intelligence pattern.
Dataflows Gen2 are a batch-oriented Power Query transformation tool; they are not a real-time analytical store or an event routing service.

questions.stem: Why should Fabric engineers review both capacity metrics and Spark/pipeline run details together?

options.text: They provide complementary views of platform pressure and workload-level behavior
options.explanation: Capacity metrics show whether the overall shared resource pool is under pressure (CU utilization, throttling) while Spark and pipeline run details show how individual jobs are behaving (stage durations, shuffle sizes, skew, task failures) — together they help engineers distinguish capacity saturation from workload-level inefficiency.

options.text: Either view alone always explains every performance issue
options.explanation: No single view fully explains every performance issue; capacity metrics may show healthy utilization while a specific Spark job has severe data skew, or a job may appear fine in isolation while the capacity is being throttled by another workload.

options.text: They replace data governance and lineage reviews
options.explanation: Performance monitoring and data governance/lineage are separate concerns; reviewing run details and capacity metrics does not substitute for Purview lineage or sensitivity label auditing.

options.text: They must both be reviewed before creating any OneLake shortcut
options.explanation: There is no requirement to review capacity metrics or run details before creating a OneLake shortcut; shortcuts are a storage configuration action independent of performance monitoring.

questions.reference: [What is the Microsoft Fabric Capacity Metrics app? - Microsoft Fabric](https://learn.microsoft.com/fabric/enterprise/metrics-app)

questions.stem: Which KQL operator is used to derive a calculated column during query execution?

options.explanation: `grant` is an access-control statement used in SQL and KQL administration to assign permissions to users or roles; it does not add or compute columns in a query result set.
`vacuum` is a Delta Lake maintenance command for removing obsolete files from a Delta table; it is not a KQL operator and does not derive calculated columns.
`restore` is used in SQL or Delta to recover data from a backup or earlier version; it is not a KQL column-derivation operator.

`extend` is the KQL operator that computes one or more new columns and appends them to each row in the result set — for example, `| extend severity_label = case(error_code > 500, 'Critical', 'Warning')` — without discarding the original columns.
